CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is organized in a standard intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and states its goals early (e.g., joint inference over object-pair and verb-frame knowledge). Notation and definitions are mostly present (O_a_{x,y}, F^a_{v t}, value set {>,<,'}) but suffer from minor inconsistencies and formatting glitches (e.g., varying spacing in F avt vs F_avt, unclear meaning of the symbol ' and occasional stray line numbers/ocr artifacts 501–599). Figures and tables are referenced usefully (Figure 3, Tables 3/4.7) and the factor graph explanation is clear, but the manuscript contains repeated sentences and duplicated fragments (several repeated paragraphs like “Thus, our work provides unique values…”) and some LaTeX/formatting remnants that hinder smooth reading.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem — recovering latent physical commonsense from text (addressing reporting bias) by learning relative attributes (size, weight, strength, rigidness, speed) of objects and verb-frame implications, which is valuable for NLP and vision/robotics (§1–2). Its framing of verb-centric frame semantics and joint inference over object-pair and frame variables via a factor graph (Sec. 4) could shift how researchers model action implications from text and complements visual approaches (noted in the conclusion and related work). By proposing a dataset, code release, and connections to existing frame resources (FrameNet/VerbNet) it opens follow-up work on richer frame knowledge and multimodal integration, making it likely to be built upon by others.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses acquiring commonsense physical knowledge from text using verb-centric frame semantics and joint inference, a topic squarely within NLP/ML (see Secs. 2–4 on frame variables F_av, object pair variables O_ax,y and the factor-graph model). The methods (factor graph combining selectional preferences, GloVe embeddings, maxent classifiers, PMI from the Google Syntax Ngrams) and evaluations (baselines, ablations, crowdsourced seeds, held-out prediction of frames and object pairs) match top-tier venue expectations. It is well positioned with respect to core resources and prior work (FrameNet/PropBank/VerbNet discussion and related work citations), targeting the right community and readership.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates against simple and sensible baselines (RANDOM, MAJORITY, and an embedding-based maxent classifier EMB-MAXENT) and reports ablations for model components (references to Table 4.7 and Table 3 for ablations), showing their model outperforms these baselines. However, although it cites several recent and relevant works on commonsense/verb physical effects (e.g., Angeli & Manning, She & Chai 2016, Gao et al. 2016, Rubinstein et al. 2015), it does not present direct experimental comparisons to those prior specialized methods or incorporate them as baselines, so comparisons are not comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (joint factor-graph inference over verb-frame and object-pair variables improves extraction of relative physical knowledge) and presents an explicit probabilistic model (definitions of F_avt and O_ax,y, unary potentials from maxent classifiers, binary “soft-1” factors, selectional preference via PMI) with empirical comparisons (baselines including EMB-MAXENT, majority, random; seed ablations). However, important methodological details are missing in the provided text (inference/training procedure for the factor graph is not specified, no mention of statistical significance testing), and the baselines are relatively weak (no stronger structured or corpus-derived competitors). These gaps leave some unsupported assumptions (e.g., how classifier probabilities are incorporated as priors and how thresholds/PMI choices affect results) that prevent full technical validation of the claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a novel frame-centric formulation that jointly models verb-induced physical implications and grounded object-pair relations via a factor graph (frame nodes F_avt and object-pair nodes Oax,y connected by selectional-preference factors ψs and cross-attribute factors ψa), which is a new perspective relative to prior resources (FrameNet/PropBank/VerbNet) and prior work cited. Although it employs established components (GloVe, PMI, log-linear classifiers, factor graphs), the joint inference across multiple physical dimensions and the explicit modeling of verb-to-object implication knowledge is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated contribution. Hence the work shows notable novelty without being a radical methodological break.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates both main tasks (predicting verb-frame implications and object-pair relations), compares to sensible baselines (RANDOM, MAJORITY, EMB-MAXENT), studies seed sizes (5% vs 20%) and reports ablations (e.g., attribute/frame similarity factors omitted and shown in ablation tables). Related work is well cited and they discuss limitations such as reporting bias and metaphorical usages and note skew in speed labels. Some reproducibility details are provided (GloVe dims, PMI source, soft-agreement potentials, selection thresholds, feature definitions), but important low-level details are missing (exact dataset splits/sizes, training hyperparameters, and inference algorithm/settings), so the experiments are solid but leave minor gaps for full replication.
